title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss the role of friendship in their lives. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Close Bonds: Four People on Friendship and Social Connections

texts.A:
### Rachel, 33, Primary School Teacher
[43]I moved to a new city in my late twenties knowing absolutely nobody, and that experience taught me just how much effort real friendship requires.[/43] I joined a book club, a running group, and a pottery class all within the same month – not because I was interested in all three, but because I was desperate to meet people. Over time, two or three genuine friendships emerged from those frantic attempts, and I now treasure them deeply. [47]I've realised that for me, a close friend is someone you can call when everything falls apart, not just someone to have fun with on a night out.[/47] [51]Since becoming a teacher, I've noticed I have less energy for superficial socialising and actively choose fewer but more meaningful connections.[/51]
texts.B:
### Omar, 45, Software Developer
[44]I work almost entirely remotely, which means friendship has become something I have to actively pursue rather than something that just happens naturally through daily contact.[/44] In my twenties I took friendships for granted – colleagues became friends, neighbours became friends, and it all seemed effortless. Now I schedule video calls with old friends the way I schedule work meetings, which felt strange at first but has actually deepened those relationships. [48]The friend I rely on most lives in another country, and we've grown closer since I started putting in that deliberate effort.[/48] [52]I sometimes worry that my children will find it harder to maintain friendships than my generation did, given how much of their social life happens online.[/52]
texts.C:
### Zoe, 27, Journalist
[45]My job means I talk to strangers every single day, so it took me a while to understand why I still felt lonely.[/45] Being sociable at work had given me a false sense of connection. It was only when a colleague pointed out that I never talked about anything personal that I realised I had been keeping everyone at arm's length. [49]I started making a conscious effort to be vulnerable with people I trusted, and the friendships I've built since then feel completely different from anything I had before.[/49] Now I actively encourage openness in my relationships even when it feels uncomfortable, because I've seen first-hand what distance costs.
texts.D:
### Mick, 58, Retired Postman
[46]When I retired two years ago, I lost the daily routine that had kept me connected to people for over three decades.[/46] My colleagues had been my social world without me fully realising it, and suddenly that world was gone. I went through a difficult period of isolation before my wife encouraged me to join a local men's group, which meets every fortnight. [50]It took several months before I felt comfortable enough to speak openly in the group, but those men have become some of the closest friends I've ever had.[/50] I think men of my generation were never taught to talk about feelings, and learning to do so later in life has changed me completely.

questions.question: had to put in considerable effort to build a social circle in an unfamiliar place?
questions.explanation: Rachel moved to a new city knowing nobody and immediately joined multiple clubs in an effort to meet people.

questions.question: finds that their working situation makes it harder to form friendships naturally?
questions.explanation: Omar works remotely and says friendship is something he now has to actively pursue rather than something that happens through daily contact.

questions.question: discovered that appearing sociable does not necessarily mean having genuine connections?
questions.explanation: Zoe's job involved talking to strangers every day, yet she still felt lonely and eventually realised she was keeping everyone at arm's length.

questions.question: found that retirement unexpectedly removed their main source of social contact?
questions.explanation: Mick lost his daily connection to colleagues when he retired, which led to a period of isolation.

questions.question: describes a specific quality they believe defines a truly close friendship?
questions.explanation: Rachel says a close friend is someone you can call when everything falls apart, not just someone to have fun with.

questions.question: mentions that making a deliberate effort actually strengthened a long-distance relationship?
questions.explanation: Omar says the friend he relies on most lives abroad and they grew closer after he started scheduling regular calls.

questions.question: describes how learning to be open about themselves transformed their friendships?
questions.explanation: Zoe made a conscious effort to be vulnerable with people she trusted, and the friendships she built since feel completely different.

questions.question: mentions that it took time before they felt able to open up to a new group of people?
questions.explanation: Mick says it took several months before he was comfortable speaking openly in the men's group.

questions.question: explains that having less free time has made them more selective about who they spend it with?
questions.explanation: Rachel says since becoming a teacher she has less energy for superficial socialising and chooses fewer but more meaningful connections.

questions.question: expresses concern about how younger people may experience friendship in the future?
questions.explanation: Omar worries that his children will find it harder to maintain friendships given how much of their social life happens online.
